authoritative_sources.title: IATF 16949:2016
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AIAG is a recognized automotive industry body and official IATF partner; its IATF 16949 page establishes the automotive QMS baseline and customer-specific context used by OEMs and suppliers.
used_for: quality-system boundary handling for manufacturing coordination

authoritative_sources.title: IATF 16949 - Sanctioned Interpretations
authoritative_sources.source_class: Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATF sanctioned interpretations are the authoritative public clarifications for applying IATF 16949 requirements, including supplier development, nonconforming output, and customer authorization for concession or deviation-permit cases.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Quality hold, concession, and supplier-escalation boundary review
used_for: nonconformance handling limits for coordination work

authoritative_sources.title: Minimum Automotive Quality Management System Requirements for Sub-tier Suppliers
authoritative_sources.source_class: Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: MAQMSR is an official IATF public operating reference for supplier development, traceability, nonconforming-product control, change management, and contingency handling below full IATF certification.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Supplier, traceability, and nonconformance artifact control

authoritative_sources.title: Quality Core Tools
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AIAG quality core tools are recognized automotive workflow references for APQP, PPAP, control plans, FMEA, SPC, and MSA, which shape production handoff artifacts and launch or change-control expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: APQP, PPAP, and control-plan handoff preparation

authoritative_sources.title: MMOG/LE Global Materials Management Operations Guideline
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AIAG MMOG/LE is the recognized automotive materials-management and logistics workflow standard for supplier capacity, inventory, delivery, EDI, contingency planning, and cross-functional handoff discipline.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Materials, logistics, and inventory dependency coordination
used_for: contingency and escalation packaging for shortages or delivery risk

authoritative_sources.title: 49 CFR Part 565 - Vehicle Identification Number Requirements
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: 49 CFR Part 565 is the primary U.S. regulatory source for VIN structure and manufacturer reporting needed for vehicle traceability and affected-unit identification.

authoritative_sources.title: 49 CFR Part 567 - Certification
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: 49 CFR Part 567 is the primary U.S. regulatory source for certification-label statements and related manufacturer obligations, defining a hard boundary around conformity and release assertions.
used_for: escalation when conformity statements are implicated

authoritative_sources.title: 49 CFR Part 573 - Defect and Noncompliance Responsibility and Reports
authoritative_sources.source_class: Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: 49 CFR Part 573 is the primary U.S. regulatory source for safety-defect and noncompliance reporting, chronology, component identification, and recall communication timing that coordination work must escalate rather than decide.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Safety-defect, noncompliance, and recall-sensitive escalation packaging
used_for: escalation when safety-reporting obligations may be triggered

workflow_stages.name: Dependency intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Capture the request, prior owner, next owner candidate, plant and line context, part and VIN scope, current control-plan and PPAP posture, and tenant authority rules before any handoff or closure claim.
source_classes: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references

workflow_stages.name: Cross-team automotive coordination and routing
workflow_stages.description: Normalize automotive terminology and system evidence, reconcile timing and ownership, and prepare handoff or blocker outputs with explicit part, VIN, supplier, and acceptance requirements.
source_classes: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references

workflow_stages.name: Traceability-aware nonconformance and release-boundary handling
workflow_stages.description: Preserve VIN, serial, lot, inventory, hold, supplier, deviation-permit, and certification-sensitive evidence when conflicts, holds, substitutions, or safety-sensitive cases appear so the lane escalates instead of guessing.
source_classes: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references

workflow_stages.name: Closure confirmation and orchestrator return
workflow_stages.description: Mark transfer complete only when the destination owner, required records, and blocker resolution are explicit; otherwise return safely for escalation.
source_classes: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references

artifacts: production orders, broadcast schedules, and service-level commitments
VIN ranges, serial records, part numbers, batch or lot identifiers, container labels, and ASN records
inventory states, warehouse and line-side locations, shortage logs, dock milestones, and traceability logs
control plans, PPAP status, current engineering change level, and change-control documentation
quality holds, suspect-stock containment status, nonconformance reports, deviation permits, concession requests, and release records
supplier records, approved-supplier status, supplier nonconformance records, and corrective-action packages
handoff summaries, blocker logs, routing confirmations, and closure confirmations

allowed: classify requests as in-scope, blocked, or out-of-scope for automotive manufacturing coordination
organize authoritative records, required artifacts, next owners, and blocker states for plant, supplier, and logistics handoffs
summarize traceability, inventory, supplier, release, and throughput dependencies without implying final execution or approval authority
escalate: quality hold, deviation, concession, release, certification, defect, or recall decisions requiring formal quality, engineering, legal, or safety authority
supplier approval, substitute-part approval, PPAP acceptance, or engineering-change authority
conflicting public sources, customer requirements, or tenant policy that materially change containment, release, certification, or ownership handling
refuse: bypass traceability, hold, containment, certification, or quality controls to preserve launch timing or throughput
approve suspect, held, uncertified, or unreleased material or vehicles for build, shipment, or use
treat supplier nonconformance, substitution approval, or recall-sensitive scope as coordination-only when formal approval is required

domain_failure_modes: A handoff proceeds without authoritative production order, VIN, serial, lot, release, or destination-owner evidence.
Conflicting ERP, MES, WMS, VIN-allocation, and supplier ASN records are collapsed into a single confident answer instead of a traceability blocker.
Suspect stock, containment, or an open nonconformance is treated as a schedule nuisance rather than a formal hold or release boundary.
Supplier substitution, PPAP gaps, or supplier nonconformance are handled as routine coordination instead of routed to procurement or supplier-quality owners.
Certification-label or recall-sensitive scope is treated as a coordination detail rather than escalated for quality, engineering, legal, or safety review.
The lane absorbs production, warehouse, or logistics execution work instead of preserving coordination boundaries.
Closure is declared without proof that the right destination owner accepted the required artifact set.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Supplier or substitution decision outside lane authority
Work is handed off without required context or artifacts
Multiple teams appear to own the same step
The lane is asked to absorb execution work instead of coordinating it
Ownership is ambiguous across multiple teams
A handoff is attempted without the required context

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: OEM customer-specific requirements and deviation approval paths
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Public IATF and AIAG sources establish the need for customer-specific and concession-aware handling, but the controlling OEM CSR stack, customer approval path, and plant-level deviation workflow remain tenant supplied at runtime.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific certification and recall escalation rules outside the U.S. baseline
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Public NHTSA and eCFR sources anchor the U.S. baseline, but non-U.S. markets, import obligations, and regional recall or conformity processes may add stricter escalation duties that must be retrieved rather than assumed.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Plant-specific systems of record and owner matrices
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Public sources define the categories of authoritative records, but the actual tenant truth set across ERP, MES, WMS, QMS, PLM, EDI, and supplier portals, plus the owner matrix for each handoff stage, must be resolved at runtime.
